content.text: Why Interface Standards Belong in Blueprints

A solution blueprint defines not only the architecture pattern and deployment topology but also the contracts between each layer of the integration. Interface standards make those contracts explicit: what shape of request callers send, what shape of prompt the service sends to Claude, what shape of data enters the context window, and what shape of response the service returns.

Without documented interface standards, each deployment of the same blueprint evolves differently. One team adds a 'priority' field to the request; another returns confidence scores in a different JSON key; a third changes the system prompt structure. These divergences break the ability to share tooling, observability, and eval suites across deployments of the same blueprint.

Interface standards are the mechanism by which a blueprint remains a blueprint across deployments. A blueprint whose interface is not standardised is just a name on a document — the actual implementations will drift apart within months.

attrs.alt: Integration interface contract layers: external, Claude, and data interfaces

content.text: Every Claude integration has three distinct interface layers. Blueprint standards address each layer separately because they have different audiences, different change cadences, and different governance requirements.
The contract between the integration and its callers (other services, frontends, scripts). Callers depend on this interface; changes require coordination.
The contract between the integration's backend service and the Claude API: the system prompt structure, the message format, the tool schemas, and the generation parameters.
The contract governing what data enters and exits the context window: the schema of the assembled prompt, the classification rules for what is permitted in context, and the token budget allocation.

The external request schema defines what callers must send. Blueprints standardise the request schema so that integrations sharing the same blueprint can share client libraries and observability tooling.

# schemas/request.py — standard request schema for synchronous chat blueprints
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import Optional
import uuid

class ConversationMessage(BaseModel):
    role: str = Field(..., pattern="^(user|assistant)$")
    content: str = Field(..., min_length=1, max_length=100_000)

class ChatRequest(BaseModel):
    # Required: identifies the integration instance and the conversation
    deployment_id: str = Field(
        ...,
        description="Identifies which blueprint deployment is being called"
    )
    conversation_id: str = Field(
        default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4()),
        description="Groups messages into a conversation for context management"
    )
    user_message: str = Field(..., min_length=1, max_length=10_000)

    # Optional: conversation history (prior turns)
    history: list[ConversationMessage] = Field(
        default_factory=list,
        max_length=20,
        description="Prior conversation turns, oldest first"
    )

    # Optional: caller-provided metadata (passed through to audit log)
    caller_context: Optional[dict] = Field(
        default=None,
        description="Opaque metadata attached to the audit log entry"
    )
The response schema defines what callers receive. All deployments of a blueprint must return the same schema, even if some fields are null when the deployment does not use them.

# schemas/response.py
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Optional
import time

class SourceDocument(BaseModel):
    doc_id: str
    title: str
    relevance_score: float

class ChatResponse(BaseModel):
    # Required fields — always present
    request_id: str                        # correlates with audit log
    response: str                          # Claude's (filtered) response
    conversation_id: str
    latency_ms: int

    # Optional fields — present only when the blueprint uses them
    retrieved_sources: list[SourceDocument] = []
    confidence_score: Optional[float] = None  # from classifier, if HITL blueprint
    review_queue_id: Optional[str] = None     # if routed to human review

    # Error envelope (present only on error responses)
    error_code: Optional[str] = None
    error_message: Optional[str] = None
    retryable: Optional[bool] = None
Blueprints define a fixed set of error codes. Callers can build robust error handling once and it works across all deployments of the blueprint.
Fix the request payload; do not retry
Refresh token; do not retry with same credentials
Reduce history length or input size; do not retry as-is
Claude did not respond within SLA
Retry once with backoff; file incident if persistent

The system prompt is the most consequential prompt the integration sends. Blueprint standards define the structural sections of the system prompt template and the order they appear in. The content of each section varies per deployment; the structure does not.

[Standard system prompt structure for RAG chat blueprints]

Section 1 — Role and scope (required, max 150 words)
  Defines who Claude is in this integration and what it can and cannot do.
  Must include: what topics are in scope, what topics are explicitly out of scope.

Section 2 — Behaviour rules (required, max 200 words)
  Explicit instructions for how Claude should behave:
  - How to handle questions outside the knowledge base
  - When to ask for clarification vs. respond with uncertainty
  - Tone and formatting expectations (bullet lists, citations, etc.)
  - What to do if the user asks Claude to ignore its instructions

Section 3 — Retrieved context (required for RAG, templated)
  Contains the retrieved documents, wrapped in XML delimiters.
  Template variable: {retrieved_context}
  Format: <document id="{doc_id}" title="{doc_title}">{doc_content}</document>

Section 4 — Output format (required, max 100 words)
  Specifies the format Claude should use for its response.
  May request structured JSON output for machine-readable responses.
  Must specify max response length guidance if relevant.
For integrations that use tool calling, blueprint standards define the naming conventions, parameter naming, and description standards that Claude uses to decide when and how to call each tool.
Blueprints specify default generation parameters and the permitted range of deployment-specific overrides. Parameters outside the permitted range require an architecture review to change.
Sequences that conflict with output format

The context window is a finite resource. Blueprint standards define a token budget for each section of the assembled prompt, ensuring that the system prompt, retrieved context, conversation history, and user message fit within the model's context window with a safety margin.

The data interface standard specifies what data classifications are permitted in each section of the context window. These rules are non-negotiable — they are validated by the security control in Component 5 of the blueprint.
Redaction runs before vector search, not after
History is stored and replayed — apply PII policy at storage time
User messages are scoped to the user's own session; not shared across sessions

When a blueprint uses structured output (JSON mode or tool use for output formatting), the output schema is part of the data interface contract. All deployments of the blueprint must use the same output schema.

# schemas/structured_output.py — used when blueprint requires JSON output
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Optional

class Citation(BaseModel):
    doc_id: str
    quote: str       # verbatim snippet from the source document
    relevance: str   # one sentence explaining why this source was cited

class StructuredChatResponse(BaseModel):
    # Claude is instructed to produce this schema via the system prompt.
    # The backend validates the response against this schema before returning.
    answer: str
    confidence: str  # "high" | "medium" | "low"
    citations: list[Citation]
    out_of_scope: bool  # true if the question could not be answered from context
    suggested_escalation: Optional[str] = None  # non-null if out_of_scope is true

# Instruction appended to system prompt when structured output is required:
STRUCTURED_OUTPUT_INSTRUCTION = (
    "Respond with a JSON object matching this schema exactly:\n"
    "{\n"
    '  "answer": "string",\n'
    '  "confidence": "high" | "medium" | "low",\n'
    '  "citations": [{"doc_id": "string", "quote": "string", "relevance": "string"}],\n'
    '  "out_of_scope": boolean,\n'
    '  "suggested_escalation": "string or null"\n'
    "}"
)
Interface standards evolve as blueprints are updated. Blueprint versioning rules determine when a change to the interface standard requires callers to update, and when it is backward compatible.
No changes to the external interface schema. System prompt wording changes, parameter tuning, internal logic improvements. Callers do not need to change.
New optional fields added to request or response schema. New optional tools added. Callers built against the prior minor version continue to work without modification.
Breaking change to the external interface: required field added, field removed, field renamed, error code removed or changed. Callers must be updated. Major version bumps require architecture review.

# interface-version.yaml — embedded in each blueprint
interface_version: "2.1.0"
external_schema_version: "2.1"
claude_interface_version: "1.3"
data_interface_version: "1.1"

# Changelog
changelog:
  "2.1.0":
    - Added optional `confidence_score` field to response schema
    - Added `CONTEXT_OVERFLOW` error code
    breaking: false
  "2.0.0":
    - Renamed `user_query` to `user_message` in request schema
    - Removed deprecated `session_id` field
    breaking: true
    migration_guide: "docs/migrations/v1-to-v2.md"
  "1.3.0":
    - Added optional `caller_context` metadata field
    breaking: false

A major version bump is an expensive event for the whole programme: every integration built on the old version must be tested and updated. The cost of a breaking change is not the blueprint change itself — it is the coordination cost across all deployments. Design for backward compatibility first; only break compatibility when the alternative is worse.

Integration interface standards are the mechanism that makes a blueprint reusable across multiple deployments without fragmentation. The three interface layers — external (what callers see), Claude (what the service sends to the model), and data (what enters and exits the context window) — each have distinct standards covering schema, versioning, error handling, and governance.

Teams adopting a blueprint get these standards pre-defined and pre-approved. Their responsibility is to follow them: use the standard request and response schemas, stay within the permitted parameter ranges, enforce the data classification rules in context assembly, and increment the version correctly when changes are made. Consistency across deployments of the same blueprint enables shared tooling, shared observability, and shared evaluation infrastructure across the programme.
